title: Flavin adenine dinucleotide

infobox.value: (**FAD**) is a [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox")-active [coenzyme](/wiki/Coenzyme "Coenzyme") associated with various [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), which is involved with several enzymatic reactions in [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism"). A [flavoprotein](/wiki/Flavoprotein "Flavoprotein") is a protein that contains a [flavin group](/wiki/Flavin_group "Flavin group"), which may be in the form of FAD or [flavin mononucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide") (FMN). Many flavoproteins are known: components of the succinate dehydrogenase complex, [α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Alpha-ketoglutarate_dehydrogenase "Alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase"), and a component of the [pyruvate dehydrogenase complex](/wiki/Pyruvate_dehydrogenase_complex "Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex").

Chemical compound

In [biochemistry](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry"), **flavin adenine dinucleotide** (**FAD**) is a [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox")-active [coenzyme](/wiki/Coenzyme "Coenzyme") associated with various [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), which is involved with several enzymatic reactions in [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism"). A [flavoprotein](/wiki/Flavoprotein "Flavoprotein") is a protein that contains a [flavin group](/wiki/Flavin_group "Flavin group"), which may be in the form of FAD or [flavin mononucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide") (FMN). Many flavoproteins are known: components of the succinate dehydrogenase complex, [α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Alpha-ketoglutarate_dehydrogenase "Alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase"), and a component of the [pyruvate dehydrogenase complex](/wiki/Pyruvate_dehydrogenase_complex "Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex").

FAD exists in two common [oxidation states](/wiki/Oxidation_states "Oxidation states"), the fully oxidized form (FAD) and the fully reduced, dihydrogenated form, FADH2. Other oxidation states also exist, including the [N-oxide](/wiki/Amine_oxide "Amine oxide") and [semiquinone](/wiki/Semiquinone "Semiquinone") states.[[1]](#cite_note-1) FAD, in its fully oxidized form, accepts two electrons and two protons to become FADH2. The semiquinone (FADH·) can be formed by either reduction of FAD or oxidation of FADH2 by accepting or donating one electron and one proton, respectively. Some proteins, however, generate and maintain a super oxidized form of the flavin cofactor, the flavin-N(5)-oxide.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Flavoproteins](/wiki/Flavoproteins "Flavoproteins") were first discovered in 1879 by separating components of cow's milk. They were initially called lactochrome due to their milky origin and yellow [pigment](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment").[[4]](#cite_note-GK10-4) It took 50 years for the scientific community to make any substantial progress in identifying the molecules responsible for the yellow pigment. The 1930s launched the field of [coenzyme](/wiki/Coenzyme "Coenzyme") research with the publication of many [flavin](/wiki/Flavin_group "Flavin group") and [nicotinamide](/wiki/Nicotinamide "Nicotinamide") derivative structures and their obligate roles in redox catalysis. German scientists [Otto Warburg](/wiki/Otto_Heinrich_Warburg "Otto Heinrich Warburg") and Walter Christian discovered a yeast derived yellow [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") required for [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration") in 1932. Their colleague [Hugo Theorell](/wiki/Hugo_Theorell "Hugo Theorell") separated this yellow enzyme into [apoenzyme](/wiki/Apoenzyme "Apoenzyme") and yellow pigment, and showed that neither the enzyme nor the pigment was capable of [oxidizing](/wiki/Oxidizing "Oxidizing") [NADH](/wiki/NADH "NADH") on their own, but mixing them together would restore activity. Theorell confirmed the pigment to be [riboflavin](/wiki/Riboflavin "Riboflavin")'s phosphate ester, [flavin mononucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide") (FMN) in 1937, which was the first direct evidence for [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") [cofactors](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)").[[5]](#cite_note-GM2-5) Warburg and Christian then found FAD to be a cofactor of [D-amino acid oxidase](/wiki/D-amino_acid_oxidase "D-amino acid oxidase") through similar experiments in 1938.[[6]](#cite_note-GM3-6) Warburg's work with linking nicotinamide to hydride transfers and the discovery of flavins paved the way for many scientists in the 40s and 50s to discover copious amounts of redox biochemistry and link them together in pathways such as the [citric acid cycle](/wiki/Citric_acid_cycle "Citric acid cycle") and [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") synthesis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Properties")]

Flavin adenine dinucleotide consists of two portions: the [adenine](/wiki/Adenine "Adenine") [nucleotide](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") ([adenosine monophosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_monophosphate "Adenosine monophosphate")) and the [flavin mononucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide") (FMN) bridged together through their [phosphate](/wiki/Phosphate "Phosphate") groups. Adenine is bound to a cyclic [ribose](/wiki/Ribose "Ribose") at the [1'](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_nomenclature "Nucleic acid nomenclature") carbon, while phosphate is bound to the ribose at the [5'](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_nomenclature "Nucleic acid nomenclature") carbon to form the adenine nucleotide. [Riboflavin](/wiki/Riboflavin "Riboflavin") is formed by a carbon-nitrogen (C-N) bond between the [isoalloxazine](/wiki/Flavin_group "Flavin group") and the [ribitol](/wiki/Ribitol "Ribitol"). The phosphate group is then bound to the terminal ribose carbon, forming a FMN. Because the bond between the isoalloxazine and the ribitol is not considered to be a [glycosidic bond](/wiki/Glycosidic_bond "Glycosidic bond"), the flavin mononucleotide is not truly a nucleotide.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This makes the dinucleotide name misleading; however, the flavin mononucleotide group is still very close to a nucleotide in its structure and chemical properties.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/FAD_to_FADH2_reduction.svg/500px-FAD_to_FADH2_reduction.svg.png)](/wiki/File:FAD_to_FADH2_reduction.svg)

Reaction of FAD to form FADH2

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Flavin_Adenine_Dinucleotide_Spectrum.png/250px-Flavin_Adenine_Dinucleotide_Spectrum.png)](/wiki/File:Flavin_Adenine_Dinucleotide_Spectrum.png)

Approximate absorption spectrum for FAD

FAD can be [reduced](/wiki/Redox "Redox") to FADH2 through the addition of 2 H+ and 2 e−. FADH2 can also be [oxidized](/wiki/Redox "Redox") by the loss of 1 H+ and 1 e− to form FADH. The FAD form can be recreated through the further loss of 1 H+ and 1 e−. FAD formation can also occur through the reduction and dehydration of flavin-N(5)-oxide.[[8]](#cite_note-Ref_1-8) Based on the oxidation state, flavins take specific colors when in [aqueous solution](/wiki/Aqueous_solution "Aqueous solution"). [Flavin-N(5)-oxide](/wiki/Amine_oxide "Amine oxide") (super oxidized) is yellow-orange, FAD (fully oxidized) is yellow, FADH (half reduced) is either blue or red based on the [pH](/wiki/PH "PH"), and the fully reduced form is colorless.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Changing the form can have a large impact on other chemical properties. For example, FAD, the fully oxidized form is subject to [nucleophilic attack](/wiki/Nucleophilic_substitution "Nucleophilic substitution"), the fully reduced form, FADH2 has high [polarizability](/wiki/Polarizability "Polarizability"), while the half reduced form is unstable in aqueous solution.[[11]](#cite_note-DS2-11) FAD is an [aromatic](/wiki/Aromatic "Aromatic") ring system, whereas FADH2 is not.[[12]](#cite_note-12) This means that FADH2 is significantly higher in energy, without the stabilization through [resonance](/wiki/Resonance "Resonance") that the aromatic structure provides. FADH2 is an energy-carrying molecule, because, once oxidized it regains aromaticity and releases the energy represented by this stabilization.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [spectroscopic](/wiki/Spectroscopy "Spectroscopy") properties of FAD and its variants allows for reaction monitoring by use of [UV-VIS absorption](/wiki/Ultraviolet-visible_spectroscopy "Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy") and [fluorescence](/wiki/Fluorescence_spectroscopy "Fluorescence spectroscopy") spectroscopies. Each form of FAD has distinct absorbance spectra, making for easy observation of changes in oxidation state.[[11]](#cite_note-DS2-11) A major local absorbance maximum for FAD is observed at 450 nm, with an extinction coefficient of 11,300 M−1 cm−1.[[13]](#cite_note-DS3-13) Flavins in general have fluorescent activity when unbound (proteins bound to flavin nucleic acid derivatives are called [flavoproteins](/wiki/Flavoprotein "Flavoprotein")). This property can be utilized when examining protein binding, observing loss of fluorescent activity when put into the bound state.[[11]](#cite_note-DS2-11) Oxidized flavins have high absorbances of about 450 nm, and fluoresce at about 515-520 nm.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9)

## Chemical states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chemical states")]

In biological systems, FAD acts as an acceptor of H+ and e− in its fully oxidized form, an acceptor or donor in the FADH form, and a donor in the reduced FADH2 form. The diagram below summarizes the potential changes that it can undergo.

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Redox_states_of_FAD.png/500px-Redox_states_of_FAD.png)](/wiki/File:Redox_states_of_FAD.png)

Along with what is seen above, other reactive forms of FAD can be formed and consumed. These reactions involve the transfer of electrons and the making/breaking of [chemical bonds](/wiki/Chemical_bonds "Chemical bonds"). Through [reaction mechanisms](/wiki/Reaction_mechanism "Reaction mechanism"), FAD is able to contribute to chemical activities within biological systems. The following pictures depict general forms of some of the actions that FAD can be involved in.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Mechanisms 1 and 2 represent [hydride](/wiki/Hydride "Hydride") gain, in which the molecule gains what amounts to be one hydride ion. Mechanisms 3 and 4 [radical formation](/wiki/Radical_(chemistry) "Radical (chemistry)") and hydride loss. Radical species contain unpaired electron atoms and are very chemically active. Hydride loss is the inverse process of the hydride gain seen before. The final two mechanisms show [nucleophilic addition](/wiki/Nucleophilic_addition "Nucleophilic addition") and a reaction using a carbon radical.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Mechanism 1. Hydride transfer occurs by addition of H+ and 2 e− | Mechanism 2. Hydride transfer by abstraction of hydride from NADH |
| Mechanism 3. Radical formation by electron abstraction | Mechanism 4. The loss of hydride to electron deficient R group |
| Mechanism 5. Use of nucleophilic addition to break R1-R2 bond | Mechanism 6. Carbon radical reacts with O2 and acid to form H2O2 |

## Biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Biosynthesis")]

FAD plays a major role as an enzyme [cofactor](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)") along with [flavin mononucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide"), another molecule originating from riboflavin.[[8]](#cite_note-Ref_1-8) Bacteria, fungi and plants can produce [riboflavin](/wiki/Riboflavin "Riboflavin"), but other [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), such as humans, have lost the ability to make it.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) Therefore, humans must obtain riboflavin, also known as vitamin B2, from dietary sources.[[14]](#cite_note-GC2-14) Riboflavin is generally ingested in the small intestine and then transported to cells via carrier proteins.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) [Riboflavin kinase](/wiki/Riboflavin_kinase "Riboflavin kinase") (EC 2.7.1.26) adds a phosphate group to riboflavin to produce flavin mononucleotide, and then [FAD synthetase](/wiki/FMN_adenylyltransferase "FMN adenylyltransferase") attaches an adenine [nucleotide](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide"); both steps require [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate").[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) Bacteria generally have one bi-functional enzyme, but [archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea") and eukaryotes usually employ two distinct enzymes.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) Current research indicates that distinct [isoforms](/wiki/Protein_isoforms "Protein isoforms") exist in the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol") and [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion").[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) It seems that FAD is synthesized in both locations and potentially transported where needed.[[11]](#cite_note-DS2-11)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Function")]

Flavoproteins utilize the unique and versatile structure of flavin moieties to catalyze difficult redox reactions. Since flavins have multiple redox states they can participate in processes that involve the transfer of either one or two electrons, hydrogen atoms, or [hydronium](/wiki/Hydronium "Hydronium") ions. The N5 and C4a of the fully oxidized flavin ring are also susceptible to [nucleophilic attack](/wiki/Nucleophilic_attack "Nucleophilic attack").[[15]](#cite_note-15) This wide variety of ionization and modification of the flavin moiety can be attributed to the isoalloxazine ring system and the ability of flavoproteins to drastically perturb the kinetic parameters of flavins upon binding, including flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The number of flavin-dependent protein encoded genes in the genome (the flavoproteome) is species dependent and can range from 0.1% - 3.5%, with humans having 90 flavoprotein encoded genes.[[16]](#cite_note-GM1.1-16) FAD is the more complex and abundant form of flavin and is reported to bind to 75% of the total flavoproteome[[16]](#cite_note-GM1.1-16) and 84% of human encoded flavoproteins.[[17]](#cite_note-Ref_7-17) Cellular concentrations of free or non-covalently bound flavins in a variety of cultured mammalian cell lines were reported for FAD (2.2-17.0 amol/cell) and FMN (0.46-3.4 amol/cell).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

FAD has a more positive [reduction potential](/wiki/Reduction_potential "Reduction potential") than [NAD+](/wiki/NAD%2B "NAD+") and is a very strong oxidizing agent. The cell utilizes this in many energetically difficult oxidation reactions such as dehydrogenation of a C-C bond to an [alkene](/wiki/Alkene "Alkene"). FAD-dependent proteins function in a large variety of metabolic pathways including electron transport, DNA repair, nucleotide biosynthesis, [beta-oxidation](/wiki/Beta_oxidation "Beta oxidation") of fatty acids, amino acid catabolism, as well as synthesis of other cofactors such as [CoA](/wiki/Coenzyme_A "Coenzyme A"), [CoQ](/wiki/CoQ "CoQ") and [heme](/wiki/Heme "Heme") groups. One well-known reaction is part of the [citric acid cycle](/wiki/Citric_acid_cycle "Citric acid cycle") (also known as the TCA or Krebs cycle); [succinate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Succinate_dehydrogenase "Succinate dehydrogenase") (complex II in the [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain")) requires covalently bound FAD to catalyze the oxidation of [succinate](/wiki/Succinate "Succinate") to [fumarate](/wiki/Fumarate "Fumarate") by coupling it with the reduction of [ubiquinone](/wiki/Ubiquinone "Ubiquinone") to [ubiquinol](/wiki/Ubiquinol "Ubiquinol").[[11]](#cite_note-DS2-11) The high-energy electrons from this oxidation are stored momentarily by reducing FAD to FADH2. FADH2 then reverts to FAD, sending its two high-energy electrons through the electron transport chain; the energy in FADH2 is enough to produce 1.5 equivalents of [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate")[[19]](#cite_note-19) by [oxidative phosphorylation](/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation "Oxidative phosphorylation"). Some redox flavoproteins non-covalently bind to FAD like [Acetyl-CoA-dehydrogenases](/wiki/Acyl_CoA_dehydrogenase "Acyl CoA dehydrogenase") which are involved in [beta-oxidation](/wiki/Beta-oxidation "Beta-oxidation") of fatty acids and catabolism of amino acids like [leucine](/wiki/Leucine "Leucine") ([isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase](/wiki/Isovaleryl-CoA_dehydrogenase "Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase")), [isoleucine](/wiki/Isoleucine "Isoleucine"), (short/branched-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase), [valine](/wiki/Valine "Valine") (isobutyryl-CoA dehydrogenase), and [lysine](/wiki/Lysine "Lysine") ([glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase](/wiki/Glutaryl-CoA_dehydrogenase "Glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase")).[[20]](#cite_note-Mansoorabadi_2007-20) Additional examples of FAD-dependent enzymes that regulate metabolism are [glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Glycerol-3-phosphate_dehydrogenase "Glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase") (triglyceride synthesis) and [xanthine oxidase](/wiki/Xanthine_oxidase "Xanthine oxidase") involved in [purine](/wiki/Purine "Purine") nucleotide catabolism.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Noncatalytic functions that FAD can play in flavoproteins include as structural roles, or involved in blue-sensitive light [photoreceptors](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") that regulate [biological clocks](/wiki/Circadian_clock "Circadian clock") and development, generation of light in [bioluminescent](/wiki/Bioluminescent "Bioluminescent") bacteria.[[20]](#cite_note-Mansoorabadi_2007-20)

## Flavoproteins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Flavoproteins")]

[Flavoproteins](/wiki/Flavoproteins "Flavoproteins") have either an [FMN](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide") or FAD molecule as a prosthetic group, this prosthetic group can be tightly bound or covalently linked. Only about 5-10% of flavoproteins have a covalently linked FAD, but these enzymes have stronger redox power.[[11]](#cite_note-DS2-11) In some instances, FAD can provide structural support for active sites or provide stabilization of intermediates during catalysis.[[20]](#cite_note-Mansoorabadi_2007-20) Based on the available structural data, the known FAD-binding sites can be divided into more than 200 types.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

90 flavoproteins are encoded in the human genome; about 84% require FAD, and around 16% require FMN, whereas 5 proteins require both to be present.[[17]](#cite_note-Ref_7-17) Flavoproteins are mainly located in the [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") because of their redox power.[[17]](#cite_note-Ref_7-17) Of all flavoproteins, 90% perform redox reactions and the other 10% are [transferases](/wiki/Transferases "Transferases"), [lyases](/wiki/Lyases "Lyases"), [isomerases](/wiki/Isomerases "Isomerases"), [ligases](/wiki/Ligases "Ligases").[[16]](#cite_note-GM1.1-16)

### Oxidation of carbon-heteroatom bonds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Oxidation of carbon-heteroatom bonds")]

#### Carbon-nitrogen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Carbon-nitrogen")]

[Monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") (MAO) is an extensively studied flavoenzyme due to its biological importance with the [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism") of [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") and [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"). MAO oxidizes primary, secondary and tertiary amines, which nonenzymatically hydrolyze from the [imine](/wiki/Imine "Imine") to [aldehyde](/wiki/Aldehyde "Aldehyde") or [ketone](/wiki/Ketone "Ketone"). Even though this class of enzyme has been extensively studied, its mechanism of action is still being debated. Two mechanisms have been proposed: a radical mechanism and a nucleophilic mechanism. The radical mechanism is less generally accepted because no spectral or [electron paramagnetic resonance](/wiki/Electron_paramagnetic_resonance "Electron paramagnetic resonance") evidence exists for the presence of a radical intermediate. The nucleophilic mechanism is more favored because it is supported by [site-directed mutagenesis](/wiki/Site-directed_mutagenesis "Site-directed mutagenesis") studies which mutated two tyrosine residues that were expected to increase the nucleophilicity of the substrates.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/MAO_mechanisms.png/960px-MAO_mechanisms.png)](/wiki/File:MAO_mechanisms.png)

#### Carbon-oxygen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Carbon-oxygen")]

[Glucose oxidase](/wiki/Glucose_oxidase "Glucose oxidase") (GOX) catalyzes the oxidation of β-D-glucose to D-glucono-δ-lactone with the simultaneous reduction of enzyme-bound flavin. GOX exists as a homodimer, with each subunit binding one FAD molecule. Crystal structures show that FAD binds in a deep pocket of the enzyme near the dimer interface. Studies showed that upon replacement of FAD with 8-hydroxy-5-carba-5-deaza FAD, the stereochemistry of the reaction was determined by reacting with the [*re* face](/wiki/Re_face "Re face") of the flavin. During turnover, the neutral and anionic semiquinones are observed which indicates a radical mechanism.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/GOX_mechanism.png/330px-GOX_mechanism.png)](/wiki/File:GOX_mechanism.png)

#### Carbon-sulfur

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Carbon-sulfur")]

Prenylcysteine lyase (PCLase) catalyzes the cleavage of prenylcysteine (a protein modification) to form an isoprenoid aldehyde and the freed cysteine residue on the protein target. The FAD is non-covalently bound to PCLase. Not many mechanistic studies have been done looking at the reactions of the flavin, but the proposed mechanism is shown below. A hydride transfer from the C1 of the prenyl moiety to FAD is proposed, resulting in the reduction of the flavin to FADH2. COformED IS a [carbocation](/wiki/Carbocation "Carbocation") that is stabilized by the neighboring sulfur atom. FADH2 then reacts with molecular oxygen to restore the oxidized enzyme.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Prenylcysteine_lyase_mechanism.png/960px-Prenylcysteine_lyase_mechanism.png)](/wiki/File:Prenylcysteine_lyase_mechanism.png)

### Carbon-carbon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Carbon-carbon")]

UDP-N-acetylenolpyruvylglucosamine Reductase (MurB) is an enzyme that catalyzes the [NADPH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate")-dependent reduction of enolpyruvyl-UDP-N-acetylglucosamine (substrate) to the corresponding D-lactyl compound UDP-N-acetylmuramic acid (product). MurB is a monomer and contains one FAD molecule. Before the substrate can be converted to product, NADPH must first reduce FAD. Once NADP+ dissociates, the substrate can bind and the reduced flavin can reduce the product.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

### Thiol/disulfide chemistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Thiol/disulfide chemistry")]

[Glutathione reductase](/wiki/Glutathione_reductase "Glutathione reductase") (GR) catalyzes the reduction of glutathione disulfide (GSSG) to glutathione (GSH). GR requires FAD and NADPH to facilitate this reaction; first a hydride must be transferred from NADPH to FAD. The reduced flavin can then act as a [nucleophile](/wiki/Nucleophile "Nucleophile") to attack the disulfide, this forms the C4a-cysteine adduct. Elimination of this adduct results in a flavin-thiolate charge-transfer complex.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Reductive_half-reaction_of_glutathione_reductase.png/960px-Reductive_half-reaction_of_glutathione_reductase.png)](/wiki/File:Reductive_half-reaction_of_glutathione_reductase.png)

### Electron transfer reactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Electron transfer reactions")]

[Cytochrome P450](/wiki/Cytochrome_P450 "Cytochrome P450") type enzymes that catalyze monooxygenase (hydroxylation) reactions are dependent on the transfer of two electrons from FAD to the P450. Two types of P450 systems are found in eukaryotes. The P450 systems that are located in the endoplasmic reticulum are dependent on a [cytochrome P-450 reductase](/wiki/Cytochrome_P-450_reductase "Cytochrome P-450 reductase") (CPR) that contains both an FAD and an [FMN](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide"). The two electrons on reduced FAD (FADH2) are transferred one at a time to FMN and then a single electron is passed from FMN to the heme of the P450.[[24]](#cite_note-1996-Hanukoglu-24)

The P450 systems that are located in the mitochondria are dependent on two electron transfer proteins: An FAD containing [adrenodoxin reductase](/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase "Adrenodoxin reductase") (AR) and a small iron-sulfur group containing protein named [adrenodoxin](/wiki/Adrenodoxin "Adrenodoxin"). FAD is embedded in the FAD-binding domain of AR.[[25]](#cite_note-1999-Ziegler-25)[[26]](#cite_note-2017-Hanukoglu-JME-26) The FAD of AR is reduced to FADH2 by transfer of two electrons from NADPH that binds in the NADP-binding domain of AR. The structure of this enzyme is highly conserved to maintain precisely the alignment of electron donor NADPH and acceptor FAD for efficient electron transfer.[[26]](#cite_note-2017-Hanukoglu-JME-26) The two electrons in reduced FAD are transferred one a time to adrenodoxin which in turn donates the single electron to the heme group of the mitochondrial P450.[[27]](#cite_note-1980-Hanukoglu-27)

The structures of the reductase of the microsomal versus reductase of the mitochondrial P450 systems are completely different and show no homology.[[24]](#cite_note-1996-Hanukoglu-24)

### Redox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Redox")]

*p*-Hydroxybenzoate hydroxylase (PHBH) catalyzes the oxygenation of *p*-hydroxybenzoate (*p*OHB) to 3,4-dihyroxybenzoate (3,4-diOHB); FAD, NADPH and molecular oxygen are all required for this reaction. NADPH first transfers a hydride equivalent to FAD, creating FADH−, and then NADP+ dissociates from the enzyme. Reduced PHBH then reacts with molecular oxygen to form the flavin-C(4a)-hydroperoxide. The flavin hydroperoxide quickly hydroxylates *p*OHB, and then eliminates water to regenerate oxidized flavin.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23) An alternative flavin-mediated oxygenation mechanism involves the use of a [flavin-N(5)-oxide](/wiki/Amine_oxide "Amine oxide") rather than a flavin-C(4a)-(hydro)peroxide.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/PHBH_mechanism.png/960px-PHBH_mechanism.png)](/wiki/File:PHBH_mechanism.png)

### Nonredox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nonredox")]

[Chorismate synthase](/wiki/Chorismate_synthase "Chorismate synthase") (CS) catalyzes the last step in the [shikimate pathway](/wiki/Shikimate_pathway "Shikimate pathway")—the formation of chorismate. Two classes of CS are known, both of which require [FMN](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide"), but are divided on their need for [NADPH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") as a reducing agent. The proposed mechanism for CS involves radical species. The radical flavin species has not been detected spectroscopically without using a substrate analogue, which suggests that it is short-lived. However, when using a fluorinated substrate, a neutral flavin semiquinone was detected.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Chorismate_synthase_mech.png/960px-Chorismate_synthase_mech.png)](/wiki/File:Chorismate_synthase_mech.png)

### Complex flavoenzymes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Complex flavoenzymes")]

Glutamate synthase catalyzes the conversion of 2-oxoglutarate into L-glutamate with L-glutamine serving as the nitrogen source for the reaction. All glutamate syntheses are iron-sulfur flavoproteins containing an iron-sulfur cluster and FMN. The three classes of glutamate syntheses are categorized based on their sequences and biochemical properties. Even though there are three classes of this enzyme, it is believed that they all operate through the same mechanism, only differing by what first reduces the FMN. The enzyme produces two glutamate molecules: one by the hydrolysis of glutamine (forming glutamate and ammonia), and the second by the ammonia produced from the first reaction attacking 2-oxoglutarate, which is reduced by FMN to glutamate.[[23]](#cite_note-Elsevier_flavo_review-23)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

### Flavoprotein-related diseases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Flavoprotein-related diseases")]

Due to the importance of [flavoproteins](/wiki/Flavoprotein "Flavoprotein"), it is unsurprising that approximately 60% of human flavoproteins cause human disease when mutated.[[17]](#cite_note-Ref_7-17) In some cases, this is due to a decreased [affinity](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry)#Receptor/ligand_binding_affinity "Ligand (biochemistry)") for FAD or FMN and so excess riboflavin intake may lessen disease symptoms, such as for [multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency](/wiki/Glutaric_acidemia_type_2 "Glutaric acidemia type 2").[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) In addition, riboflavin deficiency itself (and the resulting lack of FAD and FMN) can cause health issues.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) For example, in [ALS](/wiki/Amyotrophic_lateral_sclerosis "Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis") patients, there are decreased levels of FAD synthesis.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) Both of these paths can result in a variety of symptoms, including developmental or gastrointestinal abnormalities, faulty [fat break-down](/wiki/Fatty_acid_metabolism "Fatty acid metabolism"), [anemia](/wiki/Anemia "Anemia"), neurological problems, [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") or [heart disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine"), worsened vision and skin lesions.[[9]](#cite_note-Ref_3-9) The pharmaceutical industry therefore produces riboflavin to supplement diet in certain cases. In 2008, the global need for riboflavin was 6,000 tons per year, with production capacity of 10,000 tons.[[4]](#cite_note-GK10-4) This $150 to 500 million market is not only for medical applications, but is also used as a supplement to animal food in the agricultural industry and as a [food colorant](/wiki/Food_coloring "Food coloring").[[4]](#cite_note-GK10-4)

### Drug design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Drug design")]

New [design](/wiki/Drug_design "Drug design") of anti-bacterial medications is of continuing importance in scientific research as bacterial antibiotic resistance to common antibiotics increases. A specific metabolic protein that uses FAD ([Complex II](/wiki/Succinate_dehydrogenase "Succinate dehydrogenase")) is vital for bacterial virulence, and so targeting FAD synthesis or creating FAD analogs could be a useful area of investigation.[[28]](#cite_note-GK5-28) Already, scientists have determined the two structures FAD usually assumes once bound: either an extended or a butterfly conformation, in which the molecule essentially folds in half, resulting in the stacking of the adenine and isoalloxazine rings.[[14]](#cite_note-GC2-14) FAD imitators that are able to bind in a similar manner but do not permit protein function could be useful mechanisms of inhibiting bacterial infection.[[14]](#cite_note-GC2-14) Alternatively, drugs blocking FAD synthesis could achieve the same goal; this is especially intriguing because human and bacterial FAD synthesis relies on very different enzymes, meaning that a drug made to target bacterial FAD synthase would be unlikely to interfere with the human FAD synthase enzymes.[[29]](#cite_note-GK16-29)

### Optogenetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Optogenetics")]

[Optogenetics](/wiki/Optogenetics "Optogenetics") allows control of biological events in a non-invasive manner.[[30]](#cite_note-GK15-30) The field has advanced in recent years with a number of new tools, including those to trigger light sensitivity, such as the Blue-Light-Utilizing FAD domains (BLUF). BLUFs encode a 100 to 140 [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") sequence that was derived from photoreceptors in plants and bacteria.[[30]](#cite_note-GK15-30) Similar to other [photoreceptors](/wiki/Photoreceptor_protein "Photoreceptor protein"), the light causes structural changes in the BLUF domain that results in disruption of downstream interactions.[[30]](#cite_note-GK15-30) Current research investigates proteins with the appended BLUF domain and how different external factors can impact the proteins.[[30]](#cite_note-GK15-30)

### Treatment monitoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Treatment monitoring")]

There are a number of molecules in the body that have native [fluorescence](/wiki/Fluorescence "Fluorescence") including tryptophan, [collagen](/wiki/Collagen "Collagen"), FAD, [NADH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide") and [porphyrins](/wiki/Porphyrin "Porphyrin").[[31]](#cite_note-GK18-31) Scientists have taken advantage of this by using them to monitor disease progression or treatment effectiveness or aid in diagnosis. For instance, native fluorescence of a FAD and NADH is varied in normal tissue and [oral submucous fibrosis](/wiki/Oral_submucous_fibrosis "Oral submucous fibrosis"), which is an early sign of invasive [oral cancer](/wiki/Oral_cancer "Oral cancer").[[31]](#cite_note-GK18-31) Doctors therefore have been employing fluorescence to assist in diagnosis and monitor treatment as opposed to the standard [biopsy](/wiki/Biopsy "Biopsy").[[31]](#cite_note-GK18-31)
